steps.title: Identify what cause-and-effect requires
steps.content: To attempt to establish a cause-and-effect relationship between a treatment and an outcome, subjects must be randomly assigned to the treatment and control groups. Random assignment balances out confounding variables, so any difference in outcomes can be attributed to the treatment itself.

steps.title: Distinguish random assignment from random selection
steps.content: Random selection from the population is what lets you generalize results to that population, but it does not justify a causal claim. Random assignment to groups within the study is what makes a causal claim possible.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• A: Equal group sizes across species are not required to establish causation. Eliminated.
  • B: Randomly assigning the chipmunks and prairie dogs to treatment and control groups is exactly what is required for a causal conclusion.
  • C: Random selection from each habitat supports generalization, not causation. Eliminated.
  • D: Matching average ages across species is not required and does not by itself enable a causal claim. Eliminated.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
